1: A shadow: "At that time Herod the tetrarch heard about the fame of Jesus." Herod Antipas — son of Herod the Great, tetrarch of Galilee and Perea — hears about Jesus' ministry. The hearing is ominous: it is Herod who imprisoned John the Baptist and will soon behead him. The Christological shadow: the political power that silenced the herald will ultimately bring Jesus before a member of the Herodian dynasty at his trial (Luke 23:7-12). The king and the prophet are linked in their reception by the powers of this age.
2: A shadow: "And he said to his servants, 'This is John the Baptist. He has been raised from the dead; that is why these miraculous powers are at work in him.'" Herod's guilty conscience interprets Jesus' ministry as John the Baptist's resurrection — a theologically ironic error. He perceives resurrection power in Jesus but attributes it to the wrong person. The Christological irony: Herod is half-right; there is resurrection power at work in Jesus, but it is the power of the Son of God, not a resurrected prophet. The rumors of resurrection will become the actual resurrection that Herod and Pilate will conspire to suppress (Matthew 28:11-15).
3: A shadow: "For Herod had seized John and bound him and put him in prison for the sake of Herodias, his brother Philip's wife." The imprisonment of John — the herald who prepared the way — on account of his faithful proclamation mirrors the fate that awaits Jesus. John spoke truth to power (Matthew 14:4: "it is not lawful for you to have her"); the political-religious nexus silenced him. The pattern of the prophet silenced by power is the pattern of the passion, and John's fate is the prologue to Jesus' own trial and execution.
4: A shadow: "Because John had been saying to him, 'It is not lawful for you to have her.'" John's indictment of Herod's marriage to his brother's wife was based on Leviticus 18:16 (a man shall not uncover his brother's wife's nakedness). The faithful prophet who speaks the truth of the covenant to a ruler who violates it is the OT pattern from Nathan before David (2 Samuel 12:7) to Elijah before Ahab (1 Kings 18:18). John follows this line; Jesus will follow it more completely before Pilate.
5: A revelation of God: "And though he wanted to put him to death, he feared the people, because they held him to be a prophet." The political calculation — wanting to kill but fearing the crowds who honor the prophet — exactly mirrors the chief priests' calculation in Jerusalem (Matthew 21:46; Matthew 26:5). The pattern is not coincidental: both John and Jesus are protected temporarily by popular support, and both are eventually killed by the same combination of political pressure, private hostility, and crowd manipulation. The political mechanics of the passion are rehearsed in miniature in John's death.
6: A shadow: "But when Herod's birthday came, the daughter of Herodias danced before the company and pleased Herod." The birthday banquet that occasions the death of the prophet is a dark mirror of the eschatological banquet (Matthew 8:11; Matthew 22:1-14) — the kingdom's feast perverted into the occasion for prophetic execution. The cultural resonance of the birthday feast (a Roman custom associated with excess) contrasts with the Passover context of Jesus' death. Both prophetic deaths occur in the context of festive meals where political power overrides justice.
7: A shadow: "So that he promised with an oath to give her whatever she might ask." Herod's oath — made rashly at a banquet — mirrors the foolish oaths of the OT (Jephthah's vow, Judges 11:30-31; Saul's oath, 1 Samuel 14:24-45). The oath that cannot be undone without loss of face is the mechanism of John's death. The Christological shadow: rash oaths made in contexts of social pressure lead to prophetic deaths. Jesus warned against oaths (Matthew 5:33-37) precisely because their absolutism can override justice and wisdom.
8: A shadow: "Prompted by her mother, she said, 'Give me the head of John the Baptist here on a platter.'" The demand for the head — immediate, graphic, non-negotiable — is orchestrated by Herodias who had wanted John dead (Mark 6:19). The dance, the oath, and the demand are a chain of events in which each link was set by Herodias and executed through her daughter. The Christological shadow: the death of the prophet is engineered by those who fear the truth he speaks. The comparison with the chief priests who engineer Jesus' death through Judas is direct.
9: A shadow: "And the king was sorry, but because of his oaths and his guests he commanded it to be given." Herod's grief — he does not want John dead — but his compliance with the demand because of social pressure and the oath he cannot retract, mirrors Pilate's reluctance and compliance in Matthew 27:18-26 (he knows Jesus is innocent but gives in to the crowd's demand). The pattern of the sympathetic but complicit ruler is repeated in both deaths. Social pressure and political face overcome the private judgment of the ruler.
10: A shadow: "He sent and had John beheaded in the prison." The beheading of John — a Roman-style execution appropriate for political prisoners — concludes the prophetic career of the herald. John had announced "I am not worthy to carry his sandals" (Matthew 3:11); his death prepares the way for the one who will die publicly, on a cross, before the crowds. John's death is the private execution of the herald; Jesus' death will be the public execution of the king. Both are unjust; both serve the redemptive purpose of the Father.
11: A shadow: "His head was brought on a platter and given to the girl, and she brought it to her mother." The trophy of John's head, carried to the one who demanded it, is the final image of prophetic death as political trophy. The Christological resonance: the body of Jesus will be requested from Pilate after the crucifixion (Matthew 27:58) — the same movement of a man's body from execution to petitioner. But where Herodias requested the head as a trophy of silence, Joseph of Arimathea requests the body as an act of honor.
12: A shadow: "And his disciples came and took the body and buried it, and they went and told Jesus." The disciples' act of burial and the report to Jesus frames John's death as the immediate context for Jesus' withdrawal (Matthew 14:13). The disciples of John inform the one who John announced; the death of the herald is communicated to the one who is heralded. The Christological connection: Jesus' response to John's death (withdrawal to a desolate place) is the response of the one who knows that the herald's fate is the preview of his own.
13: A revelation of God: "Now when Jesus heard this, he withdrew from there in a boat to a desolate place by himself. But when the crowds heard it, they followed him on foot from the towns." The withdrawal after hearing of John's death is both a human response to grief and a strategic retreat before the political danger (Herod's attention has turned toward miracle-workers, Matthew 14:1-2). The crowd's persistent following — even around the lake on foot — is the measure of their need: they pursue him despite his withdrawal. His compassion for them (Matthew 14:14) overrides his own need for solitude.
14: A direct fulfillment: "When he went ashore he saw a great crowd, and he had compassion on them and healed their sick." The compassion of Jesus for the crowd — who have followed him into the wilderness without food — is the fulfillment of Ezekiel 34:16 (YHWH, the divine shepherd, healing the sick among the scattered flock). The healings in the wilderness context also echo the wilderness healings of the Exodus generation (Psalms 105:37). Christ is the divine shepherd who does not dismiss the need even when he himself has withdrawn from the crowds.
15: A type: "Now when it was evening, the disciples came to him and said, 'This is a desolate place, and the day is now over; send the crowds away to go into the villages and buy food for themselves.'" The disciples' pragmatic solution — send them away — is the human response to an impossible situation. The wilderness, the evening, the hungry crowd, and the disciples' inadequate resources are all elements of the Exodus manna narrative (Numbers 11:13: "Where am I to get meat to give to all this people?"). The stage is set for the provision that only God could make.
16: A direct revelation: "But Jesus said, 'They need not go away; you give them something to eat.'" The command to the disciples — "you give them something to eat" — places the impossible demand on them before Jesus provides through them. The Christological pattern: Christ first places the disciples in the position of inadequacy, then works through their hands. The feeding is not done by Jesus alone but through his distribution through the disciples — the pattern of the church's ministry: receiving from Christ and distributing to the hungry.
17: A type: "They said to him, 'We have only five loaves here and two fish.'" The five loaves and two fish are the concrete inventory of inadequacy — the exact measure of human insufficiency before the divine provision. The Christological bearing: Christ uses what is given, however little, and multiplies it beyond any natural possibility. The specific numbers (five loaves, two fish) are not allegorical but historical; the miracle's power is in the specificity of the inadequate provision transformed by the Son's blessing.
18: A type: "And he said, 'Bring them here to me.'" The instruction "bring them here to me" is the posture of all Christ's provision: inadequate resources brought to him become sufficient through him. The Christological principle applied to discipleship: whatever the disciple has — however little — brought to Christ becomes the material of kingdom provision. The feeding miracle teaches not just about miraculous power but about the economy of discipleship: give what you have to the Son, who multiplies it for the many.
19: A type and fulfillment: "Then he ordered the crowds to sit down on the grass, and taking the five loaves and the two fish, he looked up to heaven and said a blessing. Then he broke the loaves and gave them to the disciples, and the disciples gave them to the crowds." The four eucharistic actions — took, blessed, broke, gave — are identical to the Last Supper formula (Matthew 26:26) and the Emmaus road recognition (Luke 24:30). The Exodus feeding (manna from heaven) and the Last Supper are both anticipated here: the wilderness meal is both the fulfillment of the Exodus type and the preview of the eucharistic institution. Christ feeds the five thousand as a type of his own body broken for the world.
20: A type: "And they all ate and were satisfied. And they took up twelve baskets full of the broken pieces left over." The abundance — all satisfied, twelve baskets remaining — is the signature of the divine feeding in the OT: YHWH provides more than enough (Psalms 78:25: "the bread of the angels" that satisfied the wilderness generation; 2 Kings 4:42-44: Elisha feeding 100 with twenty loaves and having leftovers). The twelve baskets connect to the twelve disciples, the twelve tribes of Israel, and the eschatological banquet that feeds all peoples. The kingdom provision is superabundant.
21: A type: "And those who ate were about five thousand men, besides women and children." The scale of the feeding — five thousand men, plus women and children (implying perhaps 10,000-20,000 total) — establishes the miracle as a public, massive act of provision. The numbers recall the Exodus generation (600,000 men at the Exodus, Numbers 11:21); the feeding in the wilderness is the new Exodus feeding. The one who provides is the new Moses, but also more than Moses: Moses asked YHWH for meat; Jesus provides from what is given to him, by his own blessing.
22: A revelation of God: "Immediately he made the disciples get into the boat and go before him to the other side, while he dismissed the crowds." The immediate compulsion of the disciples to the boat — Jesus separating them from the crowd — mirrors John's account (John 6:15) where the crowd attempts to make Jesus king by force after the feeding. Jesus withdraws the disciples before the political pressure to become a different kind of king than he is. The Christological tension: he feeds the five thousand but refuses to be the bread-king the crowd wants.
23: A revelation of God: "And after he had dismissed the crowds, he went up on the mountain by himself to pray. When evening came, he was there alone." Jesus' retreat to the mountain for solitary prayer is the pattern of his life with the Father: the mountain as the place of divine encounter (Matthew 5:1; Matthew 17:1; Matthew 28:16). The solitary prayer in the evening mirrors Moses alone on Sinai and Elijah alone on Horeb. But Jesus does not go to the mountain to receive the law or the prophetic word; he goes as the Son who communes with the Father. Prayer is the constant rhythm of his human life with God.
24: A revelation of God: "But the boat by this time was a long way from the land, beaten by the waves, for the wind was against them." The disciples in the boat, beaten by the waves and fighting the wind, are the image of the church in its mission: making progress against resistance without the immediate visible presence of Christ. The night storm between the two feeding miracles frames the church's condition in this age: the bread has been given, the Lord has withdrawn to prayer, and the disciples are struggling against the elements until he comes.
25: A direct revelation: "And in the fourth watch of the night he came to them, walking on the sea." Walking on the sea is a divine prerogative in the OT: Job 9:8 ("who alone stretched out the heavens and trampled the waves of the sea") and Job 38:16 ("Have you entered into the springs of the sea, or walked in the recesses of the deep?") both attribute sea-walking to YHWH alone. Jesus walking on the water is a theophanic act — the display of the Creator's authority over his creation. The timing (fourth watch, 3-6 AM) is the darkest hour before dawn.
26: A direct revelation: "But when the disciples saw him walking on the sea, they were terrified, and said, 'It is a ghost!' and they cried out in fear." The disciples' terror is the standard OT response to theophany — the overwhelming of human senses before the divine presence. They misidentify the theophanic figure as a ghost (phantasma, appearance, specter) — a category error that Jesus corrects in Matthew 14:27. The fear is appropriate to the encounter; the misidentification is the failure to recognize the one they have been following.
27: A direct revelation: "But immediately Jesus spoke to them, saying, 'Take heart; it is I (egō eimi). Do not be afraid.'" The egō eimi ("I AM") is the divine self-disclosure formula of Exodus 3:14 (YHWH's name to Moses) and the Johannine "I AM" sayings (John 8:58; Matthew 18:5-6). Combined with "Do not be afraid" — the standard angel-of-the-LORD greeting (Matthew 1:20; Matthew 28:5; Genesis 26:24; Isaiah 41:10) — the verse identifies Jesus as YHWH who speaks to his people in their fear. The one walking on the water is not a ghost but the divine "I AM" in human form.
28: A direct revelation: "And Peter answered him, 'Lord, if it is you, command me to come to you on the water.'" Peter's request — "command me" — is the posture of faith in the theophanic presence. He does not presume to walk on water by his own will but asks for the word of command from the one who has authority over the water. The Christological bearing: the disciple can only do what Christ commands; Peter's walking on water is possible only because Christ says "come." The disciples' participation in the miraculous is always derivative — dependent on the word of the Lord.
29: A direct revelation: "He said, 'Come.' So Peter got out of the boat and walked on the water and came to Jesus." Peter walking on water is the miraculous participation that flows from Christ's command. The walking is real and physical; Peter's feet on the water are a participation in what Jesus is doing, by his authority. The Christological extension: the disciples are not observers of the miraculous but participants in it, when they act on Christ's word. The miracle of Peter's walking is not his own achievement but the extension of Christ's theophanic authority.
30: A revelation of God: "But when he saw the wind, he was frightened and beginning to sink he cried out, 'Lord, save me.'" The transition from walking to sinking — when Peter's attention moves from Christ to the wind — is the paradigm of faith and doubt. The wind and waves are real; the doubt is not irrational. But the one who commands the wind (Matthew 8:26) is more real than the wind. Peter's cry "Lord, save me" (Kyrie, sōson me) is the exact formula of the Psalms of lament — and it is sufficient. The Christological response: the Lord who commands the sea saves the one who sinks in it.
31: A direct revelation: "Jesus immediately reached out his hand and took hold of him, saying to him, 'O you of little faith, why did you doubt?'" The immediate hand of rescue — the same hand that healed the leper (Matthew 8:3) and raised Jairus's daughter (Matthew 9:25) — reaches into the water for the sinking disciple. The "little faith" charge is not condemnation but diagnosis and invitation: the faith that walked on water was real, and the doubt that caused the sinking is the obstacle to its completion. The rescue is unconditional; the question is the call to greater faith.
32: A direct revelation: "And when they got into the boat, the wind ceased." The stilling of the wind at Jesus' entry into the boat completes the theophanic sequence: he walks on water, he reaches for the sinking disciple, and the wind ceases at his presence. The same pattern as Matthew 8:26 — the rebuke of wind and sea — is repeated without a word: the wind ceases because he enters the space where it had been raging. His presence is the calm; his absence is the storm. The Christological principle of presence: where Christ is, the storm ceases.
33: A direct revelation: "And those in the boat worshiped him, saying, 'Truly you are the Son of God.'" The disciples' worship and confession — "truly you are the Son of God" — is the first full Christological confession in Matthew before Caesarea Philippi (Matthew 16:16). It is provoked not by teaching but by theophany: the walking on water and the stilling of the wind have disclosed what words have not yet named. The worship (prosekunēsan) is the correct response to the theophanic "I AM"; the confession is the crystallization of everything the disciples have seen. The Son of God is present.
34: A revelation of God: "And when they had crossed over, they came to land at Gennesaret." The arrival at Gennesaret — a fertile plain on the northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee — is the transition back to the public ministry of healing. The movement from the theophanic sea-crossing to the ordinary work of healing shows the pattern of Jesus' life: the mountain-prayer, the sea-walking theophany, and then the routine but compassionate healing ministry. The extraordinary revelation does not replace but undergirds the ordinary ministry.
35: A revelation of God: "And when the men of that place recognized him, they sent around to all that region and brought to him all who were sick." The recognition of Jesus at Gennesaret — and the immediate dispatch to gather the sick from the whole region — shows the cumulative effect of his healing ministry: his fame has spread, the expectation is established, and those who know of his power bring him the sick. The network of need and supply is functioning: those who have experienced his healing send for those who need it. The kingdom's reach extends through those who have received it.
36: A direct fulfillment: "And implored him that they might only touch the fringe of his garment. And as many as touched it were made well." The echoing of the hemorrhaging woman's faith (Matthew 9:21) at a regional scale — the whole region coming to touch the fringe — shows the pattern established in Matthew 9 becoming a movement. The fringe of the garment (the tassels commanded in Numbers 15:38-40) as the point of contact between the Messiah and the sick embodies the Christological logic: the covenant promise in him is the source of healing, and faith in his person is sufficient contact to receive it. The "as many as touched" is the plural form of the individual miracle.
